serving.natural_language_portion: A typical garnish serving of brined black olives is approximately 15 g (about 3–4 olives), often used as a condiment, salad topping, or mezze component.

nutrient_source.title: Black Olives, Brined — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at 0.95 confidence for all per_100g fields. Values are preserved as-is. Cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central NDB 09193 (Olives, ripe, canned, small-extra large) which reports energy 115 kcal, protein 0.84 g, fat 10.9 g, carbohydrate 6.04 g, sodium 735 mg per 100 g — confirming close alignment. HPB record treated as primary source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for brined black olives exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Olives are a very-low-carbohydrate, high-fat food. A GI of approximately 15 is assigned by analogy with other very-low-carbohydrate brined/pickled vegetables documented in Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283. Glycemic load per 15 g serving = (15 × 6.04 / 100) × 15 / 100 = 0.14, confirming negligible glycaemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 735 mg per 100 g from salt-brine curing
hypertension.guidance: Brined black olives contain 735 mg sodium per 100 g, well above the 600 mg/100 g threshold; individuals with hypertension should limit intake to a small garnish (10–15 g) and rinse olives thoroughly under running water before consumption to reduce sodium by up to 30%.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycaemic index (~15), zero sugar, MUFA-dominant fat profile
type2_diabetes.guidance: Brined black olives have negligible glycaemic impact (GI ~15, GL <0.5 per typical serving) and a MUFA-dominant fat profile that supports insulin sensitivity; they are suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes at normal garnish portions, though high sodium warrants attention in those with concurrent hypertension.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beneficial MUFA (7.9 g/100 g) and polyphenols offset by high sodium (735 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The MUFA-rich, cholesterol-free fat profile of black olives is cardioprotective, but the high sodium content from brine curing poses a cardiovascular risk at larger portions; limit to a small garnish (10–15 g) and rinse before use to balance the lipid benefits against sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 735 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g RA threshold despite MUFA/polyphenol anti-inflammatory benefits
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Although olives are inherently anti-inflammatory due to their MUFA and polyphenol content, the sodium level of 735 mg/100 g exceeds the recommended RA threshold of 400 mg/100 g; rinse thoroughly and restrict to a small garnish to minimise sodium-driven tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Brined black olives are ripe olives (Olea europaea) preserved in a salt-water brine solution, a traditional curing method widely used across the Mediterranean and Middle East, including Turkey. The brining process gives the olives their characteristic salty, slightly bitter, and rich umami flavour with a firm yet yielding texture. They are used as a mezze component, salad topping, pizza ingredient, or garnish in sandwiches and wraps. Nutritionally, brined black olives are energy-dense at 116 kcal per 100 g, driven primarily by their high monounsaturated fat content (approximately 7.9 g/100 g), which is predominantly oleic acid — the same cardioprotective fatty acid found in extra-virgin olive oil. They are cholesterol-free, very low in sugar (0 g), and contain modest dietary fibre (1.6 g/100 g). The dominant clinical concern is their very high sodium content of 735 mg per 100 g, a direct consequence of salt-brine curing, which makes them unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or rheumatoid arthritis at normal serving sizes. For people with Type 2 Diabetes, brined black olives are well-tolerated due to their negligible glycaemic index (~15) and zero sugar content. The MUFA-dominant fat profile supports cardiovascular health, though the sodium load must be managed. Rinsing olives under running water before consumption can reduce sodium content by up to 30%. Iron content is notably high at 6.28 mg/100 g, and calcium is present at 88 mg/100 g. Typical serving size is 10–20 g (3–5 olives) as a garnish or condiment.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in black olives?
Are black olives good for heart health?
Can I eat black olives if I have high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic index of black olives?
How many calories in black olives?
How to reduce sodium in canned olives?
Are brined olives high in fat?

confidence_reason: All core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database at 0.95 confidence, cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central NDB 09193 (Olives, ripe, canned) with near-identical values. Saturated fat, MUFA, and PUFA values sourced from USDA FoodData Central as supplementary detail not present in HPB record. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) as no direct measurement exists.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from 'Meat and alternatives / Beef' (input error) to 'Vegetables / Pickled and brined vegetables' — olives are a plant-based fruit/vegetable product. 2. Saturated fat (1.42 g), monounsaturated fat (7.89 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.91 g), and trans fat (0.0 g) sourced from USDA FoodData Central NDB 09193 (Olives, ripe, canned, small-extra large) as supplementary fatty acid detail not reported in the HPB record. 3. Vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and water_g are set to -1 (data not available): these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Foodo record for this item, and the USDA FoodData Central entry for canned ripe olives does not provide complete micronutrient coverage for all listed fields. 4. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for brined black olives is available in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. 5. Sugar_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source; olives contain negligible free sugars after brine curing. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-derived food with no animal-derived components. 6. Potassium reported as 8.0 mg/100 g in HPB source — this is unusually low compared to USDA (which reports ~8 mg/100 g for canned ripe olives, consistent). Value retained as reported. 7. Iron at 6.28 mg/100 g is notably high; this is consistent with HPB and USDA data for canned/brined black olives and is retained as reported.
